user_query:
We’re preparing a market entry into the US SMB payroll software segment and need a structured competitive analysis to inform positioning and first-year go-to-market. Please read all inputs in input/ and synthesize them into a rigorous, templated analysis.

Inputs to read:
- input/market_brief.txt – context on our target segment and what “Us” represents
- input/competitors.csv – the competitive set to include (name and type: Direct/Adjacent/Emerging)
- input/signals.jsonl – recent product, pricing, partnerships, hiring, funding, and incident signals by competitor
- input/pricing.tsv – simplified price positioning and tiers
- input/regions.yaml – geography and go-to-market channel presence
- input/financials.json – revenue/funding stage proxies and growth hints

Deliverables to write:
1) output/competitor_analysis_report.md
   - Use a clear structure with these section headers exactly:
     • Competitive Set
     • Competitor Profiles
     • Competitive Comparison Matrix
     • Strategic Groups
     • Key Findings
     • Strategic Implications
     • Assumptions & Data Gaps
   - In Competitive Set: include a 3-column table (Competitor | Type | Why Included) and ensure every competitor from input/competitors.csv appears.
   - In Competitor Profiles: one subsection per competitor using the Who/What/Where/How/Why/Financials template alongside Market Position, Offerings, Financial Performance, Strategy, Strengths, Weaknesses.
   - In Competitive Comparison Matrix: briefly reference the CSV you will also produce (see #2) and summarize notable gaps and leads.
   - In Strategic Groups: define at least two groups with a short label, which competitors are in each, shared characteristics, and mobility barriers.
   - In Key Findings: crisp bullets (e.g., gaps, advantages, opportunities, threats) grounded in the inputs.
   - In Strategic Implications: 5–8 prioritized, actionable recommendations for our first 12 months (what, why, expected impact, leading metric), with trade-offs.
   - In Assumptions & Data Gaps: call out uncertainties, missing data, and how they might affect conclusions.

2) output/comparison_matrix.csv
   - Comma-separated, with header columns: Dimension, Us, and one column for each competitor in input/competitors.csv (include the exact names from the file). Order of competitor columns can be any.
   - Include at least these Dimensions in the first column (exact strings):
     Market Share
     Growth Rate
     Price Position
     Product Breadth
     Quality Perception
     Innovation
     Customer Service
     Financial Strength
   - Fill cells with concise, comparable entries (e.g., High/Medium/Low, numeric ranges, or star ratings), consistent across rows.

3) output/competitor_profiles.json
   - A JSON array with one object per competitor from input/competitors.csv. For each object, include keys (all required):
     name, who, what, where, how, why, financials, market_position, offerings, capabilities, performance, strategy, strengths, weaknesses
   - Values can be strings or lists, but keys must exist.

Guidance:
- Triangulate across all input files. If evidence conflicts, note it and pick the most plausible interpretation.
- Do not fabricate precise financials or market share; use ranges or qualitative descriptors, and mark uncertainty.
- Keep the tone objective and decision-oriented.
- Make sure all file paths are exactly as specified above.
